ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic kebab food product class; no specific veg sheekh kebab subclass exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKM (Vegetable dishes) is the closest defensible code; no specific seekh kebab or Indian minced vegetable kebab code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish type.

allergens: gluten (possible from besan or binding agents)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Banarasi Veg Sheekh Kebab consists of two skewers weighing approximately 120 g, as commonly served as a starter or snack portion in Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Banarasi Veg Sheekh Kebab — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Banarasi Veg Sheekh Kebab (cr_id G00010246). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from ICMR-NIN data for comparable fried/grilled vegetable kebab preparations given HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on mixed vegetable patty / vegetable-based kebab
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Banarasi Veg Sheekh Kebab in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous mixed vegetable and legume-based patty entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The low carbohydrate content (5.24 g/100g) and high fat and protein content strongly attenuate glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per 120 g serving = (45 × 5.24 × 1.2) / 100 ≈ 2.8, rounded to 2.5 (low GL). Estimation is conservative and clinically defensible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1080 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Banarasi Veg Sheekh Kebab due to its very high sodium content of 1080 mg/100g; if consumed, limit to one small skewer and avoid accompanying high-sodium chutneys or sauces.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.24 g/100g) and low sugar (2.85 g/100g) with high protein (10.7 g/100g) supporting glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Banarasi Veg Sheekh Kebab has a low carbohydrate load and estimated low glycaemic index (~45), making it a suitable occasional choice for people with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the high sodium content warrants caution and sweet chutneys should be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (26.4 g/100g), estimated saturated fat (~7 g/100g), cholesterol (85 mg/100g), and very high sodium (1080 mg/100g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with cardiovascular disease should consume Banarasi Veg Sheekh Kebab only occasionally and in small portions, given its high fat, saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium content, all of which are established risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 1080 mg/100g exceeds the 400 mg/100g RA threshold; nightshade vegetables (green chilli, potato) present; estimated saturated fat ~7 g/100g promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit this dish due to its very high sodium content, presence of nightshade vegetables (green chilli, potato), and high saturated fat, all of which may exacerbate joint inflammation; anti-inflammatory spices (cumin, coriander, cardamom) provide partial benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Banarasi Veg Sheekh Kebab is a North Indian vegetarian grilled dish originating from Varanasi (Banaras), Uttar Pradesh, India. It is prepared by finely mincing a mixture of vegetables — typically including potato, green peas, carrots, and green chilli — combined with paneer or chickpea flour (besan) as a binding agent, and seasoned with a fragrant blend of Indian spices including cumin, coriander, cardamom, garam masala, and ginger. The mixture is moulded onto metal or bamboo skewers and grilled or roasted over a flame or in a tandoor oven, yielding a firm, slightly charred exterior with a moist, spiced interior. The flavour profile is savoury, aromatic, and mildly spicy, with earthy spice notes. In Singapore, this dish is served in Indian restaurants as a starter or snack, typically accompanied by mint chutney or tamarind sauce. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 302 kcal, 10.7 g protein, 26.4 g fat, and only 5.24 g carbohydrate, making it a low-carbohydrate, high-fat dish. Sodium is very high at 1080 mg/100g, which is a significant clinical concern for individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The low carbohydrate and estimated low glycaemic index (~45) make it relatively suitable for glycaemic management in Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without sweet accompaniments. Cholesterol is reported at 85 mg/100g, likely attributable to paneer or egg used as a binder in some preparations.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010246, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. All core macros and key micronutrients are present. Saturated fat is estimated from ICMR-NIN analogous data and clearly documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, which is the approved GI source.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB for this dish; this is a confirmed zero value per HPB source. 2. saturated_fat_g (7.0 g/100g) is estimated from ICMR-NIN data for comparable grilled vegetable kebab preparations; HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific Banarasi Veg Sheekh Kebab preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or ICMR-NIN as of 2026. 4. cholesterol_mg is reported at 85 mg/100g by HPB, which is notable for a vegetarian dish and likely reflects paneer or egg binder used in the specific preparation analysed. 5. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database.
